patient_information__preferences.what_matters_to_me:
I’m 32 years old, married, and work full-time as a middle school science teacher, so staying well enough to reliably show up for my students and for my family is what matters most to me. My asthma has been around since I was a kid, but over the last few years I’ve realized that if I don’t stay on top of my controller inhaler and avoid my triggers, I lose entire days to wheezing, coughing, and feeling exhausted. I want to be able to:

1) Play actively with my 4‑year‑old daughter (running around at the park, kicking a soccer ball, going on short hikes) without constantly having to stop to catch my breath.
2) Teach a full day of classes without needing to step out to use my inhaler or being embarrassed by coughing fits in front of my students.
3) Sleep through the night without waking up wheezing or feeling tight in my chest, because broken sleep really affects my mood and patience the next day.
4) Keep exercising regularly (I like jogging and light weight training) so I can maintain a healthy weight and reduce stress, but I want to do it in a way that doesn’t trigger my asthma.

I’m willing to stay consistent with my controller medications and make lifestyle changes (like using my air purifier, closing the windows on high‑pollen days, and rinsing my nose after being outdoors) if it means fewer flare‑ups. I also care about understanding exactly what each medication does and having a clear, written plan so that when my breathing starts to get worse, I’m not panicking or guessing—I can follow the steps and know when it’s time to call my doctor or go to urgent care. Ultimately, what matters to me is staying healthy enough to be present, dependable, and active for my family, my students, and myself, without asthma silently controlling what I can and can’t do every day.

triggers__usual_symptoms.other_trigger:
Primary triggers for me are:
- Seasonal pollen (especially in the spring and early fall—oak and grass pollen seem to be the worst; I usually start feeling tightness and sneezing when the pollen count is high on the weather reports).
- Dust and dust mites (cleaning out closets or moving old boxes often sets me off; I’ve noticed that vacuuming without a mask makes me start coughing and wheezing within an hour).
- Cat dander (my sister has two cats; if I visit and stay more than an hour without taking precautions, my nose stuffs up and my chest gets tight).
- Respiratory infections, especially colds picked up from students (a simple cold often turns into a week or more of worsened asthma with more frequent reliever use).
- Cold air, especially when I go running outside in the winter or walk quickly from the car to the house on very cold mornings.
- Strong chemical smells and aerosols (bleach cleaners, some air fresheners, and certain perfumes; I’ve had coughing fits when a coworker sprayed a strong air freshener in the staff room).
- Smoke of any kind (cigarette smoke, wildfire smoke, and even heavy grill smoke from barbecues). During wildfire season, being outside for even 10–15 minutes without a mask can make me wheeze later that night.

I try to minimize these triggers by checking daily air quality and pollen reports, using a HEPA air purifier in our bedroom, washing bedding weekly in hot water, and avoiding visiting my sister’s apartment if my asthma has already been a little unstable. I also carry a mask for cleaning dusty areas or going out on high‑smoke or high‑pollen days.
triggers__usual_symptoms.other_usual_symptoms:
My usual asthma symptoms, when things are starting to get out of control but before it’s a full‑blown attack, include:
- A tight, squeezing feeling in my chest that makes it feel like I can’t get a full breath in, especially when I try to take a deep breath.
- Wheezing, mostly on exhalation, that my wife can sometimes hear when we’re lying in bed and the room is quiet.
- A dry, persistent cough that gets worse at night or when I’m laughing, talking for a long time in class, or walking up stairs.
- Shortness of breath with fairly normal activities, like carrying groceries up one flight of stairs or walking quickly across the school campus between classes.
- Feeling unusually tired and winded after mild exercise, like a slow 20‑minute jog that I can do easily when my asthma is well controlled.
- Occasionally, mild chest discomfort that is not sharp, more like a dull pressure that improves after using my reliever inhaler.

When I’m having a more significant flare‑up, I will sometimes:
- Wake up at 2–3 a.m. coughing and feeling like I need to sit up to breathe more comfortably.
- Need my reliever inhaler every few hours instead of not at all or just before exercise.
- Notice that my peak flow (when I remember to check it) drops by around 20–30% from my personal best.

These symptoms usually build up over a day or two, especially if I’ve had a cold or been around a strong trigger like pollen or smoke, so recognizing them early is important for me to start my action plan and increase my controller dose or seek help if needed.

daily_medications___controller__reliever.controller_1_name_colour_strength: Fluticasone propionate (Flovent HFA) – orange inhaler, 110 mcg
daily_medications___controller__reliever.controller_2_name_colour_strength: Montelukast – 10 mg tablet, taken by mouth
daily_medications___controller__reliever.reliever_name_colour_strength: Albuterol sulfate (Ventolin HFA) – blue inhaler, 90 mcg per puff

stay_controlled__avoid_my_triggers.stay_controlled_1_take_amount: 2 puffs of Flovent 110 mcg twice daily
stay_controlled__avoid_my_triggers.stay_controlled_2_take_amount: 1 tablet of Montelukast 10 mg once daily at night

take_action.continue_this_dose_for_first_section: Continue this increased controller dose for 7–10 days or until symptoms are back to baseline for at least 48 hours, then step back down to the regular STAY CONTROLLED doses as instructed by my doctor.
take_action.take_action_1_take_amount_first_section: Increase Flovent 110 mcg to 4 puffs in the morning and 4 puffs in the evening
take_action.take_action_2_take_amount_first_section: Use Albuterol 90 mcg 2 puffs every 4–6 hours as needed for cough, wheeze, or chest tightness (max 8 puffs in 24 hours without calling doctor)
take_action.take_action_3_take_amount_first_section: Start using a spacer with my inhalers every time, and schedule more frequent rest breaks during physical activity; avoid heavy exercise until symptoms improve.
take_action.continue_this_dose_for_second_section: Use this emergency reliever plan only for severe symptoms or as directed by urgent care/ER. Call 911 or go to the emergency department immediately if I need to repeat this more than once in 4 hours or if I am struggling to speak in full sentences.

notes__completion.notes:
Additional context and self‑management notes:

- Personal best peak flow: approximately 540 L/min (measured on multiple good days). I start to worry and consider this a yellow zone if my peak flow drops below about 430–450 L/min, and a red zone if it is below 350 L/min.
- Typical early warning signs for me include: needing my albuterol more than 3 times a week (not counting pre‑exercise use), waking up coughing or wheezing more than once a week, or noticing that I feel out of breath walking up one flight of stairs that is usually easy for me.
- I keep one albuterol inhaler at home (bedroom nightstand), one in my work bag at school, and one as a backup in the car glove compartment. I set a calendar reminder to check expiration dates every 3 months.
- I try to use a spacer (AeroChamber) with both my controller and reliever inhalers to improve medication delivery. I rinse my mouth and spit after using my Flovent to reduce the risk of oral thrush.
- During cold and flu season, I am extra cautious because upper respiratory infections have led to my worst asthma flare‑ups in the past. If I start to get a cold (sore throat, runny nose, or mild fever), I increase monitoring of symptoms, use my peak flow meter daily, and am more consistent about saline nasal rinses and using a humidifier at night.
- Environmental controls at home: We use hypoallergenic pillow and mattress covers, wash bedding weekly in hot water, and run a HEPA air purifier in the bedroom overnight. We do not allow smoking in the house or car. We try to keep windows closed on high‑pollen or high‑pollution days and use the air conditioner instead.
- Work considerations: I’ve talked to my principal and the school nurse about my asthma. The nurse has an extra inhaler spacer for me, and my colleagues know that strong fragrances and aerosol sprays in shared spaces can trigger my symptoms. I have permission to keep my inhaler with me in the classroom at all times.
- Emergency plan: If I am using my albuterol every 2–3 hours with only short‑term relief (less than 3–4 hours), if my lips or fingernails look bluish, if I can’t speak in full sentences without stopping for breath, or if my peak flow is in the red zone, my wife will drive me to the nearest emergency department or we will call 911. We have this plan written on a card on the fridge.

This plan is meant to be reviewed and updated at least once a year, or sooner if I have a hospitalization, a course of oral steroids, or a major change in symptoms or triggers.
notes__completion.completed_with: Dr. Melissa Chen, MD – Pulmonology, Northwest Asthma & Allergy Clinic
